response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend several hours daily on social media

response_parsed.opinion: I spend quite a bit of time on social media these days, and honestly, the amount of fake stuff I see is overwhelming. At my age, I have seen a lot in life, but this whole internet thing with people making up stories and spreading lies is something else entirely. Working retail in New York, I talk to customers every day, and I can tell you that people believe the wildest things they read online without questioning anything. It worries me because I see how it affects folks, making them angry or scared over things that are not even real. When I come across something that seems too dramatic or too good to be true, I try to slow down and think about it. Does this really make sense? Who is saying this and why? I have learned that if something makes me feel really strong emotions right away, that is usually a warning sign. The fake content makers know how to push our buttons. I stick to sources I recognize and trust, like the major news networks I have been watching for decades. If I see something shocking from a page I have never heard of, I just scroll past it. I do not have the energy to investigate everything, and frankly, with my education background, I know I am not always equipped to figure out what is real and what is not. My faith teaches me to seek truth and not bear false witness, so I try not to share things unless I am pretty sure they are legitimate. Sometimes I ask my coworkers what they think, since we chat during breaks anyway. The depression I deal with makes spending hours online both a comfort and a trap, because the negativity and fake stories can really drag me down. I have started limiting which pages I follow and trying to focus more on my painting and gardening instead. It is not a perfect system, but at seventy-seven, I am doing my best to navigate this confusing digital world without getting taken in by every piece of nonsense that comes across my screen.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is a 77-year-old transgender woman working full-time in retail in New York with lower secondary education. She spends several hours daily on social media and deals with depression. Her low openness suggests preference for familiar, traditional approaches. Moderate conscientiousness indicates she tries to be careful but may not always follow through systematically. Her Christian faith would emphasize truth-telling. Her age and education level suggest she may find digital literacy challenging but has life experience to draw on. The tone should be straightforward, somewhat world-weary, practical, and honest about limitations. She would rely on established institutions, personal judgment based on experience, and emotional awareness rather than sophisticated fact-checking techniques.
response_parsed.tone: straightforward, world-weary, practical, honest about personal limitations, conversational
